chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: CSCE 477/477-Honors / CSCE 703
Course Title: Cybersecurity Risk
Section: 477: 200, 500. 703: 600
Time: Tue-Thu 3:55 pm to 5:10 pm
Location: EABB-106
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor: Shreyas Kumar
Office: PETR 107
Phone: 979-458-6860
E-Mail: shreyas.kumar@tamu.edu
Office Hours: Wed 2 p.m. - 3 p.m. or by appointment – in person or via Zoom.

chunks.title: Course Description
Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
For 703: CSE Graduate classification and a curious mind
For 477: Grade of C or better in CYBR 201/CSCE 201, CSCE 201/CYBR 201, or CSCE 221; junior or senior
classification.
For 477-Honors: Honors students, Junior or Senior classification, or Honors student + Instructor's
permission

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Demonstrate an understanding of the core concepts and practices that underpin cybersecurity risk
assessment.
• Describe advanced and emerging cybersecurity risk issues, including supply chain security, privacy
risks, and critical infrastructure protection.
• Integrate course concepts to assess, classify, and prioritize risks by completing hands-on risk
assessments using realistic case studies.
• Communicate the role of cybersecurity risk within enterprise risk management and deliver actionable
insights with strategic recommendations.
• Apply real-world risk management approaches in real-life scenarios, demonstrating practical reasoning
and cybersecurity risk knowledge.
• Honors section:
Ο Develop the project into a research paper/research talk/poster
Ο Learn to apply Cybersecurity Risk concepts to a small business

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Computer Security: Principles and Practice – William Stallings, 4th Edition, Pearson, 2018. [Optional]
Texas A&M University at Qatar faculty are encouraged to use available online resources or adopt
multiyear textbooks to limit challenges encountered with shipping and receiving textbooks.

chunks.title: Description of Graded Work
chunks.content:
The student's semester grade will be based on assignments, papers, exams, class attendance, and class
participation. This course includes extensive in-class hands-on "hacking/solving" type exercises. Class
attendance is essential for student success; therefore, students are required to promptly and regularly
attend all their classes. A record of attendance will be maintained from the first day of classes and/or
the first day the student's name appears on the roster through final examinations and will contribute to
the participation grade for the course. Absences may only be excused as defined by the Texas A&M
University Student Rules available at http://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07. This course is stacked with
CSCE 703. Graduate students should enroll in those courses rather than CSCE 477. In addition to all
undergraduate (CSCE 477) work, graduate students will have additional requirements for the Semester
Paper (i.e., longer paper, more references).
Students are also expected to actively participate in class activities, including Q&A sessions and
discussions, to enhance their understanding and engagement with the course material.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Out of 1000 assignable points:
• A = 900-1000 points
• B = 800-899 points
• C = 700-799 points
• D = 600-699 points
• F = 599 points and below

| Requirement: | Points |
|---|---|
| 5 Assignments | 400 |
| 3 individual @ 60 pts each | |
| 2 group @ 110 pts each | |
| Participation | 100 |
| Open-book exam | 300 |
| Student Research poster | 50 (477 H) |
| Semester Project/Risk Assessment | 200 |
| Total | 1000 |

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late penalty: 1 point (out of 100) per hour. Late submissions will not be accepted 4 days after the
deadline and/or after the solution has been posted.
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is
exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Weekly Modules and Course Coverage (Check the Table for actual dates)
• Introduction
Ο Overview of course objectives and expectations
Ο Introduction to Cybersecurity Risk Management Concepts
• Risk Analysis, Password Cracking
Ο Hands-on password-cracking techniques and tools
Ο Analyzing risk scenarios related to weak authentication
• Risk Analysis, Frameworks
Ο Deep dive into NIST Cybersecurity Framework and FEDRAMP templates
Ο Applying risk assessment frameworks to real-world scenarios
• Data Protection and Data Breaches
Ο Understanding data protection strategies and compliance requirements
Ο Case studies on major data breaches and lessons learned
• Secure Design and Coding
Ο Principles of secure software design and coding best practices
Ο Identifying and mitigating common coding vulnerabilities
• Offensive Security
Ο Introduction to ethical hacking and penetration testing
Ο Conducting vulnerability assessments on sample systems
• Encryption Algorithms
Ο Exploring symmetric and asymmetric encryption techniques
Ο Hands-on with RSA, AES, and encryption in practice
• Risk Management via Threat Modeling
Ο Building threat models using frameworks like STRIDE
Ο Analyzing attack surfaces and mitigation strategies
• Security Compliance
Ο Navigating compliance standards like GDPR, HIPAA, and ISO 27001
Ο Mapping compliance requirements to enterprise security practices
• Cybersecurity Risk Management Tools
Ο Practical use of tools like NIST's Cybersecurity Framework tools
Ο Evaluating risk management software and automation solutions
• Cybersecurity Law and Privacy
Ο Key legal principles and regulations in cybersecurity
Ο Exploring privacy frameworks and data governance
• Risk Management via People, Process, Tech
Ο The human factor in cybersecurity: awareness and training
Ο Implementing processes and technologies to reduce risk
• Risk Management in the Future
Ο AI and Cybersecurity
Ο Lifelong Learning
• In-class open-book exam
Ο Continued project presentations and peer feedback
Ο Final reflections and course wrap-up

chunks.title: University Policies
chunks.content:
This section outlines the university-level policies that must be included in each course syllabus. The TAMU
Faculty Senate established the wording of these policies.
NOTE: Faculty members should not change the written statements. A faculty member may add separate
paragraphs if additional information is needed.

chunks.title: Campus-Specific Policies
chunks.content:
Campuses may establish their own policies and minimum syllabus requirements. As long as these policies
and requirements do not contradict the university level requirements, Campuses can add them in this
section. Please remove this section if not needed.

chunks.title: College and Department Policies
chunks.content:
College and departmental units may establish their own policies and minimum syllabus requirements. As
long as these policies and requirements do not contradict the university level requirements, colleges and
departments can add them in this section. Please remove this section if not needed.

chunks.title: Artificial Intelligence Statement
chunks.content:
• With the emergence of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, the ways in which we define our
creative processes continue to transform. AI generators are rapidly evolving from simple editing
for grammatical errors and spelling mistakes (Grammarly, MS Word Spell Check) to
sophisticated text production (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.), as well as image, computer code,
and audio generation. The presence of such tools, however, does not replace our need to learn
how to draft, revise, and reflect on texts, programs, drawings and how to exercise information
literacy and personal responsibility in how we locate, evaluate, incorporate, and cite primary/
secondary sources. For example, the Association for Writing Across the Curriculum states the
following: Writing to learn is an intellectual activity that is crucial to the cognitive and social
development of learners and writers. This vital activity cannot be replaced by AI language
generators (AWAC).
Engaging in the various aspects of creative pursuits (e.g., writing, coding, drawing) is critical to education
in a broad sense. While AI technologies will continue shaping how we approach these creative tasks, the
critical work of creativity relies on integrity, originality, and ethical conduct in regard to appropriate
representation as an author or creator. Thus, submitting work with a significant percentage of AI-
generated content, unless otherwise permitted, can be considered academic misconduct under Texas
A&M University Student Rule 20. Students must therefore cite the use of Generative AI tools and
document what they have contributed to an assignment.
The following may lead to F* grade in this class
• Use of AI tools in assignments or exams where AI is disallowed
• Use of AI tools in assignments for any real customers who prohibit AI usage
• Sharing an AI account for the purpose of work done for this course
• Using AI in ways not allowed. Example Final exam may specify AI is allowed, but taking or
uploading photo prompts are not allowed.
• Misrepresenting AI usage when asked to.
